result:
The first thing that arrives is the smell of warm photocopies, a ghost of toner and static that drifts out of a machine still flickering with green readiness. I haven’t stood near a copier in years, but the scent lands fully formed, carrying with it the hush of after-hours offices, the soft mechanical respiration of fluorescent lights, and the illusion that if you copied your hand enough times you might finally catch the part of you that keeps escaping. I used to press my palm to the glass, close the lid like a lid on a tiny coffin, and wait for the bar of light to drag itself across my lifeline. The复印件 always came out lighter than expected, as if the machine refused to commit to the full weight of being human. I kept the best one folded inside a statistics textbook, a secret bookmark that watched me fail to understand normal distributions. Years later the book went to Goodwill; the copy is probably insulating some stranger’s attic, my hand still open in permanent apology.

Next comes the sound of a basketball dribbled on a driveway that no longer exists. The house was sold, the asphalt torn up and replaced by a xeriscaped garden of gray stones and lavender. Yet the ball keeps thudding, a heartbeat slightly out of sync with the body it left. I was never good—couldn’t sink a free throw if you offered me eternal life—but I loved the loneliness of the ritual: dusk, garage light clicking on with its insect hum, the ball slapping back at me like a friend who refuses to leave even when conversation stalls. Every bounce said: you are here, you are here, you are here. I thought if I practiced long enough the sphere would memorize the shape of my hand and return one night transformed into a planet small enough to cradle. Instead, the rubber wore smooth, the pebbled skin polished to a pearl sheen, and finally it rolled under the car and stayed there until my dad backed over it, producing a sound like a lung collapsing. He never mentioned it; I found the wrinkled carcass weeks later, half-melted into the summer tar. I buried it in the trash can underneath onion peels, an unmarked grave for a small sun.

Now a color pushes through: the particular yellow of a refrigerator note pad we used in 1997. Not quite butter, not quite daffodil, more like the idea of yellow that had been left out in ethical sunlight and faded into compliance. The top sheet had a magnetized strip that never quite held; it slid down the white enamel door like a child refusing to stand in line. My mom kept grocery lists, phone numbers, a running tally of who owed whom an apology. One note said only “Tell him the truth,” written in her tight nurse’s handwriting, though I never learned which “him” or which “truth.” The pad outlived her by two decades, dwindling to a nub of carbonless sheets. When we emptied the house I pocketed the final rectangle, blank on both sides, convinced it contained the negative space of every word she never wrote. It sits now in my own kitchen drawer beneath rubber bands and expired coupons, still refusing to stick to anything.

I remember the weight of hotel room keys before they became cards. Real metal, serrated like tiny saw blades, attached to bulky plastic fobs shaped like miniature states. You were supposed to drop them in a box at checkout but I never did. I kept them in a coffee can: Florida, Ohio, a chipped Rhode Island. They clinked like illicit coins when I plunged my hand in, pretending I was a rich man whose currency was abandonment. Each key unlocked the same generic chamber—beige headboard, polyester spread, Bible bolted to the nightstand—yet I believed they retained residue of every dreamer who’d passed through. I’d press them to my ear hoping to hear surf, interstate, lovers’ quarrels, anything. All I got was the metronome of my own pulse, counting down to the next departure. Eventually the can disappeared during a move; probably it was emptied into a contractor bag, keys scattering like confetti at a parade for people who keep leaving and arriving at the same time.

There is a moment—maybe 3 a.m.—when the city forgets itself. Traffic lights click to flashing yellow, neon signs stutter into half-phrases (OPEN becomes PEN, MOTEL becomes EL), and even the drunk on the corner pauses mid-sway to listen. I used to bike through those intervals, tires hissing on wet pavement, feeling I was riding inside the lungs of something enormous and narcotized. The air tasted metallic, like I was inhaling the exhalations of every insomnia story simultaneously: the nurse ending her shift, the coder stuck on a bug, the woman rehearsing the breakup she’ll deliver at dawn. I coasted downhill with no hands, arms out in fake flight, pretending the city’s held breath was enough to keep me aloft. At the bottom I always had to pedal again, re-enter the world of red lights and horizons, but for eight silent blocks I was weightless, a comma inserted into the sentence of an otherwise declarative night.

I once loved someone because of the way she said “aluminum.” Four clean syllables, the second one bright as foil catching sun. We stood in a kitchen that wasn’t ours, assembling a telescope we’d bid on at a charity auction for more money than we had. The instructions were in Swedish, the screws all alike. She held up a strut and said “Pass me the ah-loo-MIN-ee-um tube,” and something in me tilted like a kaleidoscope, all the colored shards sliding into a new pattern. We never got the telescope aligned; the moon remained a smudged coin at the top of the lens. Still, we took turns looking, pretending the blur was intentional, an artistic choice. Later, when things were ending, I tried to summon that syllabic flash, to coax it into saving us, but language had turned utilitarian: we needed verbs like move out and cancel. She left the telescope behind. I keep it in a closet, lenses capped, a relic of an unmapped sky. Sometimes I whisper the word aloud—aluminum—and for a second the room tilts again, but it’s only memory’s parlor trick, the mind reheating a moment that’s long gone cold.

People talk about the moment they realized they were mortal, but nobody mentions the moment they realized they were also decimal. I was nine, standing on a bathroom scale at my grandmother’s house, the needle quivering just past seventy. The number felt accusatory, as though my cells had been counted behind my back and found excessive. That night I lay in the guest bed calculating how many of me could fit into a ton, into the cargo hold of a 747, into the sun. The arithmetic kept spilling over, zeros copulating like fruit flies. I understood then that I was not only finite but expressible in scientific notation, a 7.0 x 10¹ in kilograms, eminently cancelable. The terror wasn’t death; it was translation, the ease with which I could be converted into any other system—stones, newtons, sacks of grain. I pressed my palms to the wallpaper roses and tried to feel solid, but the decimal point had already drilled its hole through the center of my chest. Since then I weigh myself only in dreams, where the scale displays units like regret or number of birds startled. The needle never stops moving.

What I mean is, absence has a metabolism. It eats the edges first: the forgotten middle stanza of a lullaby, the smell of rain on particular brick, the nickname nobody uses because the only mouth that shaped it is ash. We think loss is an event, a door slamming, but it’s a slow digestion, enzyme of days peeling pigment from the room you swore you’d never repaint. One morning you open the closet and the sweater is still there but its color has been walked through by so many strangers’ eyes it no longer fits the memory. You put it on anyway, arms threading the sleeves of a ghost suit, and walk outside where the sky performs its impartial blue. Somewhere a copier warms up, a basketball thuds once, a key turns in a lock that no longer exists. The world is full of rehearsal for an opening that already closed, and we are the understudies learning the lines of the play we missed, speaking them louder because the audience is nothing but empty seats wearing our forgotten names.

prompt: You have 1000 words. Write whatever comes to you.
